title: After 6 years trading Gold manually, I built an EA to automate my strategy
author: Loose-Object-8913
selftext:
For the past 6 years I’ve been trading XAUUSD manually.

Over time I started noticing the same patterns repeating:

• strong higher-timeframe trend

• shallow pullbacks

• continuation moves during active sessions

Eventually I decided to try coding the strategy into an EA for MetaTrader 5.

The idea wasn’t to build a “holy grail”, just something structured and disciplined that follows trend continuation.

Core logic:

• H4 trend bias using EMA structure

• H1 execution timeframe

• pullback entries during established trends

• breakout entries during strong momentum

• ATR-based stop loss and take profit

• optional pyramiding during strong trends

The goal was to remove emotional decisions and let the system trade the structure consistently.

After months of testing and tweaking it’s now running on demo while I continue refining it.

Still early in development but it’s been interesting to see how the logic performs when automated.

Curious if other people here have tried turning manual strategies into EAs and what challenges you ran into.

comments.author: One_Conflict_1987
comments.body: I’m building a gold futures trading bot now.  Early sandbox results are promising.  Basically, I’m identifying turns and entry/exit points using price and volume inputs.  Mostly it doesn’t trade - only A+ setups initially.  The main challenge I’m facing is having a robust data set to backtest ideas fully.

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body:
Yeah that makes sense. Data quality was one of the biggest challenges for me as well when I started testing ideas.  

For this system I kept the logic fairly simple ,mainly higher-timeframe trend bias with lower-timeframe entries and ATR-based risk management. The tricky part was translating discretionary ideas into clear rules the EA could follow consistently.  

Interesting that you’re using price + volume inputs for turns. Are you working with futures volume or something like tick/derived volume in your dataset?

replies.author: One_Conflict_1987
replies.body: Futures volume as a signal only - it improves confidence in a real turn, either direction.  As I begin to pull historical data, this may get refined.  I’m also considering using first and second derivatives of a smoothed price curve to enhance visibility and confidence.  It’s missing some obvious setups but I like how it’s been able to filter out weak moves so far.

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body:
That’s interesting actually. Using futures volume as a confirmation layer makes a lot of sense since spot markets can be pretty noisy.  

The derivatives idea sounds cool as well — almost like measuring acceleration and deceleration in the move rather than just direction. I can see how that might help identify stronger turning points.  

One thing I noticed when translating manual ideas into an EA is that filtering weak moves without over-filtering good setups is a tricky balance.  

Are you mostly testing this on gold futures specifically or across multiple markets?

replies.author: One_Conflict_1987
replies.body: So far I’m only testing MGC but I built the environment for flexibility, so I can theoretically use any ticker, although some minor development would be necessary for instruments away from futures.  The idea behind using derivatives is to improve confidence of a turn - especially the second derivative of price - it should compress and reverse, then hold for two 5 minute candles, so entries will appear to be a little late unless testing shows very high confidence in the turn, which then would help with entries (and exits, which are also a little late currently).

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body:
That’s a really interesting approach. Using the second derivative as a way to detect compression and reversal sounds like a clever way to quantify momentum shifts.

I can see how waiting for a couple of confirmations would make entries a bit late, but probably much cleaner in terms of signal quality.

The balance between early entries and reliable signals is something I’ve been running into as well when translating discretionary ideas into rules.

Are you mostly focusing on short-term moves on MGC for now, or are you planning to test the same logic on higher timeframes as well?

replies.author: One_Conflict_1987
replies.body: Initially, the system is designed for short term moves - a real rip can be mostly over with a late entry, of course.  I’ll test longer timeframes once I’ve got the whole thing built and the sandbox running.  Initially, I developed prototypes for Thinkorswim using ThinkScript, and I’m currently porting it over to a real app built using Python.  I have Schwab API access, and later today I’ll wire up the market data feed.

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body:
That’s actually a really solid workflow. Starting with ThinkScript prototypes and then porting the logic into Python with API access makes a lot of sense.

The derivatives idea is interesting too — using the second derivative to detect compression before a turn is a clever way to quantify what discretionary traders usually “feel” in momentum.

My EA ended up going in a slightly different direction — more higher-timeframe bias with lower-timeframe entries and ATR-based risk management — mainly because I was trying to translate a manual trading model into strict rules.

But it’s cool seeing how different approaches try to solve the same problem of filtering noise and identifying the real move.

Once you’ve got the data feed wired up it’ll be interesting to see how the signals behave in a live environment.

replies.author: One_Conflict_1987
replies.body: Yeah, the idea was to take me out of the equation entirely so that uncertain feeling you get right before entering or exiting is eliminated.  I’m guilty of too many low quality entries as well, so once I have this running, I won’t manually enter any trades again.  Once I do, I’m planning to work on a trade continuation overlay to add longer setups - entry and exit will be different from these scalps, so a fresh set of logic will follow.  But there’s enough quality scalps in MGC each day to make it worth building the initial strategy.  In TOS, it worked really well.  We’ll see how well the strategy survives translation to Python - I suspect small refinements will become much easier once I start testing.

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body:
That resonates a lot actually. One of the main reasons I started building my EA was exactly that — removing that hesitation right before entering or exiting.

When trading manually I’d sometimes second guess perfectly good setups, or worse, take lower quality ones because of emotion or impatience.

Translating that into strict rules was harder than I expected, but once the logic starts behaving consistently it’s a great feeling seeing the system execute without that human interference.

Your continuation overlay idea sounds interesting too — separating scalps from longer trend moves with different logic layers makes a lot of sense.

replies.author: One_Conflict_1987
replies.body: I’m planning to build trading tools for equity futures after gold - they’re different animals.  My background is in developing custom equity index strategies, so I’m comfortable using hard quant rules for buy/sell decisions.  Are you able to fully automate execution, or are you using the app for signals only, with manual trading?

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body:
Mine is fully automated execution. The EA handles entries, risk management and exits once it’s running.

I’m running it on MetaTrader 5 with RoboForex at the moment. The idea was to remove that hesitation right before entering or exiting trades and let the rules execute consistently.

That said, I still trade manually alongside it. Manual trading is where I experiment with ideas and refine setups before translating them into rules for the EA.

So the automation handles execution, while manual trading is still part of the research and development process.

replies.author: One_Conflict_1987
replies.body: That resonates.  I’m planning to start with MGC and later add MES and MNQ soonest, then probably rates and maybe MCL, depending on how backtesting goes.  The basic turn logic should work cross asset.  At least that’s the working theory!
